speaker: interviewer
text: --nee Steinitz. We were talking about you hiding with your friend after your sister had been sent Ober Alstadt. Pesach--

speaker: interviewee
text: First night of Passover. My mother says, stay indoors. All winter you are sleeping, hiding in the barn in that cold. I hear a rumor that they don't need anybody anymore. The camps are full. And so I listened to my mother, like a good girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: The labor camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And in the middle of the night, banging on the door, yelling and screaming outside. And my mother opened the door and they took me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister's gone. My father stand still. My mother, they cannot protect her. The youngest child is taken away. There is nothing they can do. And I'll never forget the look in my father's eyes. He came to be reunited with us. And now, one by one, is being taken away. The next day, they marched us to the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There must have been about 80 or 100, I don't really know. And the mothers were standing on the sidewalk to wave their last goodbyes. And some of them, most of them, would throw little parcels. My mother threw something at me and I picked it up. And when we came to a holding camp in a neighboring town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- we opened it, what the parents gave us. So some mothers sent the kids acid, muriatic acid, with a little note. Put it on your hands or your feet. Then when the doctor examines you, if you are capable of working, and he'll see all those burns, he'll send you home. My mother gave me a bottle of what we called here Tylenol or aspirin. Called Togal at that time. I think the Germans still manufacture it. And she asked me in the little note to take it all at once. So if I go-- the doctor examines me, if I am capable of working, and my heartbeat won't be regular. Or maybe I'll faint, and he'll send me back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had a decision to make. Do I obey my mother? Could I go ahead and go to camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid to take the pills all at once. I wanted to live. And I was going to take my chances. I was 16. And wanting to live, somehow, was more important to me at that point than listening to my mother's advice. The first time that I didn't listen to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see your father again after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I am. The girls that did obey their mothers or do what they asked them to do were sent back home. Because at that time, they could still get more people. And it wasn't total annihilation yet. Remember, it was shortly after the 20th January Wannsee Conference. They didn't have that, that machinery in place. So they did send them back because there was more to come. And I stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't sure that I made the right decision. I know now I made the right decision. Because in June of the same year, they deported the whole ghetto. And almost nobody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they deported to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Auschwitz. And all my-- all the young women that were with me that sent-- were sent home went directly to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you asked me if I saw my father. I got a note smuggled into this holding camp where they kept us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the holding camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Sosnowiec. And it was a durchgangslager, which means a holding camp in German. And I got a note smuggled in through someone that my father wants to see me. And he's going to be at such and such time on the roof across the street, because that used to be a factory in the middle of town. And that he would like to see me. And so I went to the window, which was with iron bars. And I saw him wildly waving to me standing on the roof. I don't know if he saw me. You cannot see somebody in the darkness of a room. I saw him the last time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While we were there, we were asked to do-- clean up the place. They didn't provide maids for us. And I was asked to wash the floor in the corridor. And as mother told us, she said if you want to have a maid, you have to know how to do things. That's how I was taught at home. And I got on my knees and I'm washing the floor. And suddenly, I hear boots. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it's beyond description the fear that grabs you. You know that these boots belong to either a guard or to the commander of the camp. You don't know if you should continue to do your work. If you should work faster. If you slow down. If you should get up. At this point, this moment lasts a lifetime. And you'll never forget that fear. And as these steps come closer, suddenly I see him stop right next to me. And you don't know if he's going to take a gun and shoot you. If he's going to kick you, beat you. You continue working like nothing is happening around you. And suddenly he says, you are a good worker and walks away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I continue to wash the floor. And I came up with a scheme.

speaker: interviewer
text: I found out he was the commander of the camp. And I want to be together with my sister. And I went down where I-- where his office was, found out that, too. Knocked on the door and he didn't know who was knocking. And he says, [NON-ENGLISH] And I walk in. And he sees me. I, I was surprised he didn't fall off the chair. A Jewess to come into his office? And so he barks at me, what do you want. And what are you doing here? I said, I wanted to ask you a favor. He looks at me. He says, are you the one that was wash-- that washed the floor yesterday? I, I said, yes. He says, what do you want? I said, I'd like to go to the camp where my sister is. I know where my sister is, and I want to be reunited with my sister. Where is she? And I tell him, Ober Alstadt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says, you are stupid. You're such a good worker. I'm going to send you to a men's camp. You'll work in the kitchen. And you will have food. And you won't work hard. And you'll have it much better than in a women's camp. In a women's camp, you'll work seven days a week. And you will starve. You want to die just to be with your sister? I said yes, we'll die together. I want to be with my sister. Do you remember the name of the Kommandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [? Novack. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: An SS--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oberscharfuhrer [? Novack. ?] And he was a murderer. And everybody feared him. But you see, if they were approached with a certain chutzpah, you-- that was a gamble. But when you're 16 and you have nothing to lose, you just do whatever comes to your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, OK. I see what I can do. And I said to him, my girlfriend is here, too, and she has a sister there also. He says, get out of here. This was the friend that I was hiding with. She was caught the same time. Now, next day, they lined us all up. It was like a slave market. And he was marching, and he says 10 go here and 20 go there. And as he's walking in front of me, I said, excuse me? I ask you to be sent to my sister. Oh, yes. Step out. I said, and my friend, too. He looks at me and he says, out. Now, you don't know what is on his mind. Is he going to shoot you for the chutzpah of even asking and bothering him? You have-- you don't know. But you don't care. You don't care. You take your chances.

speaker: interviewee
text: He counted up a few people. And lo and behold, we were sent to Sudetenland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we-- by-- in train. By train. With guards. So we arrived there in the evening. And I am-- if you can call it happy in a time like this. I'm gratified that what I did worked and that I'm going to be united with my sister, and that my parents are going to be happy that at least we are together. We arrive in the camp in the evening as the girls were coming from work. And I find out that I am not in that camp where my sister is. And I can't describe my disappointment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp where you found yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to a big camp called Parschnitz. Also, a spinning mill. The women just came from work. They lined up for their food. There is a lot of dust in a spinning mill because you break the fiber of the flax. Today, we call it marijuana. This is what we were working with. This is the fibers that they make linen. And there is so much dust that you're covered. You're gray. I see all these gray faces. Their clothes, their hair, everything covered with, with the dust. And they stand with their little containers from metal for their food. And the Jewish girl, what we call Judenalteste, with a horsewhip walking up and down the line. And if anyone was talking or not standing straight in line, she would beat them. This was my introduction to a camp. This is when I realized where I am and what was happening. This is where I realized that I'm no longer free, that I do not have a name, that I am a number. And I have no freedom to choose what I want to do, where I want to go. It was a sad day in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your number, Mrs. Frieberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think after the war, I was so determined to put it all behind me for the first few weeks, that I just didn't bother to. I found out-- as I said, I was a nosy kid. I was still nosy-- that we're not staying. Not all of us stay in this camp. That the next day, there will be some women commandants coming from the surrounding areas to make an exchange. You see, this is very beginning. There were girls that got sick in the camps or had accidents in the factory and were sent back home. We were told they were sent back home. Whether they were sent home or in Auschwitz, I have no idea. We don't know. But they were sent back. And they were coming from the surrounding areas. So the next morning when they started to arrive, I was looking for the commandant from the camp where my sister was. And I was asking them. To use my fluent German, I had a bit of an edge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I found that commandant. And I said, I am-- I have a sister in your camp. She said, are you Gerda? I said, yes. How did you know my name? She said, because your sister told me that whenever I'm bringing some more women into the camp, I should look for you. She was sure that one day you'll show up, that there's no way that they will leave you to stay at home with your parents. Why would she have even the nerve to ask her to look for me? This was a peasant woman that-- because being a Nazi got this position and commandant in the camp. Never in her life did she have a manicure. But my sister was a hairdresser. And she was doing her manicure. And while she was doing this, she asked her to look for me. You say she was a peasant? She was a German peasant?

speaker: interviewee
text: German, yeah. German woman. Of course, a German woman, a Nazi, who got this position.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember her name anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember her name anymore. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so I found myself in my sister's camp. And of course, my friend as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a fenced camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived, we were still-- there was only 90 women. And we were still living in the factory. We slept on bunk beds and-- in the factory. We never left the factory. But then as we arrived, the owner of the factory was made to build barracks and a fenced-in camp with barbed wire. And right after, it became no longer a labor camp, but a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the owner of the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ignatz [INAUDIBLE]. And so now we moved from the factory to the camp, which was about 2 kilometers or so from the factory. Because he built that camp on the edge of a reservoir. And so we walked to work and from work every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were from wooden barracks. Not heated. There was no stove or any heating in them. There was no running water in the barracks. There were no wash rooms there. There was one barrack that had a pipe with a little faucet into one trough, like, and there was-- like, a lot of girls could wash their hands. And there were no showers or anything like that. And the same for the toilets. There were no single toilets. It was toilets like for a number of women at the same time. And at night or so, you just had to go out in the winter at night. If you had to go, you had to go. In three years that I was there, we never had warm water. We never got a piece of soap or toothpaste. We never think of these things. People say they were in camp, but we don't think of what we were deprived of being there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who supervised the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this commander. And then we had what we called a Judenalteste. And then there were guards that would march with us to work and watch us at work. But we were supervised by the German foremen in the factory while we were working. They had nothing to do. You see, the owner of the factory paid the SS for the slave laborers. He was responsible for our lodging. He was responsible for our food. All the costs incurred he was responsible for. And he paid the wages to the SS. That was the agreement when they-- that they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were only women in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were only women. And the camp was a satellite of the camp Gross-Rosen. And all around the area, there were other small camps, all spinning mills because this was-- the, the yarns were needed for tents and uniforms. So it was a real-- a war-related industry at the time. And of course, they would assign you where you were working. You did not have a choice. But I guess that the foreman would watch you and see, more or less, how you work and where you work. And it was always the fear of them thinking that you sabotage. There was always the fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- you were trying to put out as much as possible. If the thread that went through the machine-- and I worked on a machine with 120 spools, the largest machine on the floor. And when it-- they were breaking all the time. So you were hurrying up to tie it up so-- because all the spools had to be the same size. So you rushed to have it. Because if they would see that one spool isn't turning, they would immediately beat you up because they would think that, well, you sabotaged it. So this threat was always hanging over your head. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The machines were getting old. They were machines from Great Britain. Because there was a war, they got no parts. And any time a machine broke, they accused us of sabotage. So I started to repair my own machine when it-- any time something went wrong with it, whatever I could do to make it go again, I would do it. And I always tried to prevent the machine from breaking. I knew what caused the breaks. And I found out what caused the break-up. So I would prevent it. They weren't stupid. They noticed it. They knew exactly what I was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to those girls whose parts broke down and were accused of sabotage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were beaten up right in the factory. Their food was taken away from them for a day or two. They had ways of punishing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They cut their hair, shaved their heads. Because at that time, we did not have shaved heads. We also did not have striped uniforms. We were wearing our clothes, whatever we came in. That's what we wore every day. And so they very quickly realized that I am very handy with-- mechanically inclined. And because all the men were in the war, they only had old men in their machine shop. And they begin to make their own parts. So they put me in the machine shop, and I started to work on a [? lathe ?] under supervision and-- making parts.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only woman there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No women at all. I was the only one. And I was the only Jewish slave working in that-- in that machine shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the other workers were the local German--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Czech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Czech.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czech. Because again, Sudenten is like Silesia. Sudeten was German and Czech. And I was the only one that worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, I did not have an SS card because it was too expensive. And where am I-- where am I going to run?

speaker: interviewee
text: We plotted an escape for years, my sister and I. But we couldn't leave my mother behind. It was difficult for three people to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your mother arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: A year later. So we always plotted an escape. But it was not easy. So they-- the closest to what we could do is we decided that we want-- I wanted my mother to know where I am and that we are together. And because of something that happened in the factory, they did not allow us the monthly letter that we were allowed to write. So I came up with an idea that I will write to Berlin where my aunt still was. See, the Jews in Berlin stayed very-- quite a long time there. They were the last ones to go because they did not like this publicity. Because there, they really were in Germany. So they dealt with the German Jews in the big cities last. My aunt still was home in Berlin. And so I cleaned a machine for a German-- for a Czech woman. And I asked her for a stamp. And that's what she did. She gave me a stamp. And I wrote a letter, addressed it to my aunt in Berlin. And now, foolishly I was going to mail this letter myself. And so I figured out when I go from the night shift in the dark and there is no moonlight, I will go last in the column. And when the SS guard moves up to see if everybody marches, as she leaves the end of the column, I'll run across, mail the letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was walking around with the letter for a number of days, till one day I had the opportunity to do so. Only that the next day, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] the commander of the camp calls me into her office and shows me the letter. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guy that was working the post office, a Nazi, saw an address that he has never seen in that village. Berlin. Schindler. It was my mother's sister, Rose Schindler. She lived in Berlin. It was strange to him that the address did not have a, a sender. It was just an address where it was going. So he figured out it must be from camp. He took the letter, which is amazing because a letter once mailed does not belong to anyone. It belongs to the state. It is the property of the post office. He brought it back to camp and showed it to the commander.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kommandant.

speaker: interviewee
text: They opened the letter. And she read it, and she knew it was my letter. And she called me in and showed me the letter. And all I wrote in that letter was to my aunt that I cannot write to my mother, but I know that she's worried. So she took that letter and took that Judenalteste with her and went to the city to the SS headquarters. And said to the commander there, to the oberscharfuhrer, that she has somebody in her camp that has stepped out of bound. And that she'd like him to send her away to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this commander says, so what should she do? And she said, well, she sent a letter. He says, show me the letter. He reads the letter. The reason I know the story is because the Judenalteste was with her, and then she told me what happened. He says to her, woman, what do you want to do to this child? All she wants is her mother to know that she is alive. Is that a crime? For this, you want to send her to Auschwitz? Go back to your camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I only know it because the Judenalteste was with her. So here you have a peculiar situation. An SS officer, a Nazi-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a second time-- is acting humanely. So if we say that they were crazy or they were deranged to do what they did to the Jews, it's very difficult to say this. They were human. The only thing was they followed the orders of a vicious leadership. But deep inside, some of them had some humanity left. And I think that this SS man maybe had a son on the front. And maybe he didn't hear from him. And maybe he was waiting for a letter. And maybe that's why I'm alive. You never know. And so that was my episode with sending a letter to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were working in the factory and you had contact, was there any rumors that came through these non-Jewish local Czechs/Polish about the war, what has happening to the Jews in the other camps, the extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- we knew that there was other camp. But let me tell you first, so we don't lose the, the continuity of the story. While we were-- I was in camp. My sister was in camp. In June of 1942, they deported the whole ghetto of Jaworzno.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother found herself with my father and with her in-laws, with my grandmother and her husband, on the truck to be shipped to Auschwitz. By coincidence with the parents of my friends, with the two girls that we were together, and while waiting for the convoy to move, the mother of my friend said to my mother, if-- I hope we all survive. But in case I don't, would you be a mother to my children? And if you wouldn't survive and I do, I will do the same for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Among the screams and cries, mothers looking for the children, babies being tossed around, people being beaten, old people not being able to get on those trucks, in that whole confusion, in that whole tragedy that was unfolding, it got a little dark. They came to an end. My mother pushed my father off the truck. She jumped after him. And they ran away and hid in the Jewish cemetery. The convoys left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this near where they were in Jaworzno?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jaworzno. The convoy left with the people. With my father's mother. He left her behind. These were situations, these were decisions that Jews had to make. He left his mother behind and jumped with his wife to save his life, to maybe be together, reunited, with his children. And so his mother went with the transport to Auschwitz. That was the only destination at the time. And he hid with his wife in the cemetery, only to be found two days later by the Jewish police. It was raining while they were out there. And they were picked up by the police. They found them. They were searching all the homes and everywhere where Jews possibly could have hid.
